title: Dog Knee Popping In and Out: Understanding Patellar Luxation
meta_desc: Is your dog's knee popping? Learn about patellar luxation, when to see a vet, and how to help your pet in our veterinary triage guide.
content_html:
What is Patellar Luxation?

When a dog's knee appears to pop in and out of place, it is most commonly a condition known as patellar luxation. This occurs when the patella (kneecap), which normally sits in a groove at the end of the femur, slips out of its track. This can happen due to genetic factors, such as a shallow groove or malaligned limb, or as a result of trauma. You may notice your dog 'skipping' or running on three legs for a few steps before the knee snaps back into place and they return to normal activity.

Urgency Level: Medium

A popping knee is generally considered a Medium urgency level. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a progressive condition that causes discomfort and leads to the development of osteoarthritis over time. You should schedule a veterinary exam within the next 24 to 48 hours. However, if your dog is suddenly non-weight-bearing on the limb, vocalizing in pain, or if there is significant swelling, you should contact an emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing a video of your dog moving is one of the most helpful tools for a veterinary triage team. Because patellar luxation is intermittent, your dog may walk perfectly fine once they arrive at the clinic due to adrenaline or the kneecap temporarily being in the correct position. A video of the 'skipping' behavior helps the vet determine the frequency and severity of the episodes, which is vital for accurate grading and treatment planning.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of a luxating patella?
faqs.answer: Common signs include a sudden 'skip' in the gait, holding a hind leg up for several steps, an occasional limp, or a popping sound when the joint moves.

faqs.question: Are certain dog breeds more prone to this condition?
faqs.answer: Yes, small and toy breeds such as Chihuahuas, Pomeranians, Yorkshire Terriers, and Poodles are most commonly affected, though it can occur in large breeds as well.

faqs.question: Can patellar luxation be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Mild cases (Grade 1) may be managed with weight management, physical therapy, and joint supplements. More severe cases usually require surgical intervention to prevent chronic pain and permanent joint damage.
